ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper offers notable novelty by proposing a unified sentence-pair (f2) framework, releasing an open-source integration (dataset-sts / KeraSTS), and introducing harder Answer Sentence Selection datasets (yodaqa/large2470 and wqmprop) plus a statistically grounded evaluation protocol (averaged runs with 95% CIs). Its demonstration that transfer learning from the large Ubuntu Dialogue dataset improves performance across diverse f2 tasks further supports a new, task-independent perspective. However, the core models evaluated are existing architectures and several important model classes/datasets were left out, so the work is more a meaningful consolidation and infrastructure contribution than a fundamentally new modeling breakthrough. | B(4): The paper contributes a notable and useful perspective by unifying diverse sentence-pair semantic tasks under a single f2 framework, providing an open-source toolkit, stronger baselines, and new harder datasets (yodaqa/large2470, wqmprop) along with statistically grounded evaluation (averaged runs with 95% CIs). It also empirically demonstrates transfer learning benefits from the large Ubuntu Dialogue dataset, supporting model reusability across tasks. However, the core modeling techniques are standard (CNN/RNN/attention hybrids and IR baselines) and do not introduce fundamentally new algorithms, so the contribution is more integrative and benchmarking-focused than a major methodological breakthrough.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper offers notable novelty by proposing a unified sentence-pair (f2) framework, releasing an open-source integration (dataset-sts / KeraSTS), and introducing harder Answer Sentence Selection datasets (yodaqa/large2470 and wqmprop) plus a statistically grounded evaluation protocol (averaged runs with 95% CIs). Its demonstration that transfer learning from the large Ubuntu Dialogue dataset improves performance across diverse f2 tasks further supports a new, task-independent perspective. However, the core models evaluated are existing architectures and several important model classes/datasets were left out, so the work is more a meaningful consolidation and infrastructure contribution than a fundamentally new modeling breakthrough.

It also empirically demonstrates transfer learning benefits from the large Ubuntu Dialogue dataset, supporting model reusability across tasks. APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=5] A(5): Yes — the paper addresses a core NLP/ML topic (sentence-pair semantics/text comprehension) and is squarely within the scope of top conferences, presenting experiments on standard benchmarks (SNLI, SICK, STS) plus new and diverse datasets (Ubuntu, wang, yodaqa/large2470, wqmprop). It targets the right audience by comparing classical IR baselines and multiple neural architectures, introducing a unified open-source framework, stronger baselines, and a statistically grounded evaluation protocol (averaged runs with 95% CIs). The problem formulation, methods (CNN/RNN/attention hybrids, transfer learning) and evaluation metrics are appropriate and positioned well for the venue’s community. | B(5): Yes — the paper addresses a core NLP/ML area (sentence-pair semantics) and aims to unify tasks under an f2 framework, which is squarely within top-conference scope. Its audience is appropriate (researchers working on sentence-pair modeling and transfer learning) and the problem formulation (task-independent models evaluated across multiple datasets) suits venue expectations. The methods and evaluation meet community standards: multiple neural architectures, IR baselines, new harder datasets (yodaqa/large2470, wqmprop), statistically grounded evaluation (averaging runs and 95% CIs), and open-source code. The paper is positioned correctly in the research community by comparing to established baselines, reporting hyperparameters, and demonstrating transfer learning from a large Ubuntu Dialogue dataset.

reviewer_a.justification: Yes — the paper addresses a core NLP/ML topic (sentence-pair semantics/text comprehension) and is squarely within the scope of top conferences, presenting experiments on standard benchmarks (SNLI, SICK, STS) plus new and diverse datasets (Ubuntu, wang, yodaqa/large2470, wqmprop). It targets the right audience by comparing classical IR baselines and multiple neural architectures, introducing a unified open-source framework, stronger baselines, and a statistically grounded evaluation protocol (averaged runs with 95% CIs). The problem formulation, methods (CNN/RNN/attention hybrids, transfer learning) and evaluation metrics are appropriate and positioned well for the venue’s community.

reviewer_b.justification: Yes — the paper addresses a core NLP/ML area (sentence-pair semantics) and aims to unify tasks under an f2 framework, which is squarely within top-conference scope. Its audience is appropriate (researchers working on sentence-pair modeling and transfer learning) and the problem formulation (task-independent models evaluated across multiple datasets) suits venue expectations. The methods and evaluation meet community standards: multiple neural architectures, IR baselines, new harder datasets (yodaqa/large2470, wqmprop), statistically grounded evaluation (averaging runs and 95% CIs), and open-source code. SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper runs broad experiments across many f2 tasks and datasets (including new harder answer-selection sets), compares IR baselines and multiple neural architectures, introduces transfer-learning from Ubuntu Dialogue, and uses a statistically grounded evaluation (averaged runs with 95% CIs). However, explicit ablation or sensitivity analyses are not reported and several important model classes and tasks (e.g., serial RNN attention variants, skip‑thoughts, additional datasets) are left out, leaving minor gaps in analysis. Reproducibility is well supported (code and hyperparameters provided) and the authors discuss failures and dataset limitations, supporting a solid but not exhaustive evaluation. | B(4): The paper runs a broad set of experiments across many sentence-pair tasks and baselines (TF-IDF, BM25, avg, DAN, CNN, RNN, RNN-CNN, attention) including new harder datasets (yodaqa/large2470, wqmprop), transfer learning from the Ubuntu Dialogue corpus, and reports averaged results over 16 runs with 95% CIs—supporting the main claims. However, it lacks explicit ablation studies or sensitivity analyses of key architectural choices (e.g., many attention/RNN variants and skip-thoughts are omitted), leaving some causal attributions underexamined. Related work coverage is described as adequate, and limitations (dataset issues, overfitting, tasks left for future work) are discussed. Reproducibility is strong: code and hyperparameters are provided.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper runs broad experiments across many f2 tasks and datasets (including new harder answer-selection sets), compares IR baselines and multiple neural architectures, introduces transfer-learning from Ubuntu Dialogue, and uses a statistically grounded evaluation (averaged runs with 95% CIs). However, explicit ablation or sensitivity analyses are not reported and several important model classes and tasks (e.g., serial RNN attention variants, skip‑thoughts, additional datasets) are left out, leaving minor gaps in analysis. Reproducibility is well supported (code and hyperparameters provided) and the authors discuss failures and dataset limitations, supporting a solid but not exhaustive evaluation.

However, it lacks explicit ablation studies or sensitivity analyses of key architectural choices (e.g., many attention/RNN variants and skip-thoughts are omitted), leaving some causal attributions underexamined. Related work coverage is described as adequate, and limitations (dataset issues, overfitting, tasks left for future work) are discussed. Reproducibility is strong: code and hyperparameters are provided.

REPLICABILITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper reports hyperparameters (stated as True) and provides code, and it defines evaluation metrics/procedures precisely (MAP/MRR/R@k/Pearson and averaging runs with 95% CIs). Most datasets are standard and the authors introduce new datasets, and they provide an open-source framework which aids reproduction. Missing/unclear aspects from the summary: the data preprocessing pipeline is not explicitly described here and the summary does not confirm whether the newly introduced datasets are publicly released in full, so a few minor details needed for exact replication may be absent. | B(4): The paper reports hyperparameters and model configurations (Hyperparameters reported: True) and provides code, which supports reimplementation. Evaluation metrics and procedures are precisely described (MAP/MRR/R@k/Pearson, averaging over 16 runs and reporting 95% CIs). However, the summary does not clearly confirm a fully detailed preprocessing pipeline or explicit public availability/packaging of the newly introduced datasets, leaving minor reproduction gaps. Overall, most details are present but a few dataset/preprocessing specifics appear missing.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper evaluates a wide set of baselines (TF-IDF, BM25, avg embeddings, DAN, CNN, RNN, RNN-CNN, attention) across many sentence-pair datasets using consistent metrics and a statistically grounded evaluation protocol (averaged runs with 95% CIs), which supports fair comparisons. The authors explicitly acknowledge when they lag behind state-of-the-art on RTE and STS and demonstrate transfer-learning gains, indicating honest reporting of prior methods’ strengths. However, they omit several important model classes and some tasks/datasets (e.g., serial RNN attention variants, skip-thoughts, baBi, AskUbuntu), so comparisons are strong but not fully comprehensive. | B(4): The paper compares a broad set of baselines (TF-IDF, BM25) and many neural architectures (avg embeddings, DAN, bidirectional GRU RNN, CNN, RNN-CNN hybrid, attention) across numerous datasets (wang, yodaqa/large2470, wqmprop, Ubuntu v2, SNLI, SICK, STS, WebQuestions, moviesE) using consistent metrics and statistically grounded evaluation (averaging 16 runs and reporting 95% CIs); code and hyperparameters are provided. It also acknowledges when prior/SOTA methods outperform its models and reports limitations. However, it omits some important model classes (e.g., serial RNNs with attention variants and skip-thoughts) and leaves several tasks for future work, so comparisons are strong but not fully comprehensive.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is well organized with a clear methodological flow (introduction of the unified f2 framework, description of models and datasets, experiments, and conclusions) and the main contributions (new datasets, unified evaluation framework, and transfer learning results) are stated early. Notation and experimental details appear consistently reported (hyperparameters, metrics, averaging over 16 runs with 95% CIs), which aids reproducibility. Tables/figures and examples seem effective insofar as they present robust baseline comparisons and confidence intervals, though the summary indicates some model classes and tasks were omitted—this could have been framed more explicitly to clarify scope. Overall the writing quality is good, with only minor presentation/scope-clarity issues. | B(4): The paper is well-organized and presents a logical flow from motivation to methodology and experiments, clearly stating its main contributions early (a unified f2 framework, new datasets yodaqa/large2470 and wqmprop, statistically grounded evaluation with averaged runs and 95% CIs, and an open-source framework). Experimental notation and details appear consistent and reproducible (hyperparameters reported), and the comparative tables/figures across baselines and architectures are informative. Writing quality is good and largely free of ambiguity, with only minor presentation issues (e.g., some model classes and datasets are left for future work) that do not substantially harm clarity.

IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper addresses an important practical problem by unifying diverse sentence-pair tasks under a single f2 framework and providing an open-source platform (dataset-sts / KeraSTS), new harder Answer Sentence Selection datasets (yodaqa/large2470, wqmprop), and a statistically grounded evaluation protocol (averaging runs with 95% CIs). Its systematic comparison of classical IR baselines and multiple neural architectures, plus the demonstration that transfer learning from the large Ubuntu Dialogue dataset improves performance, will likely change experimental practice and encourage model reuse across tasks. It is not fully transformative because the models do not achieve SOTA on RTE/STS and some model classes/datasets were omitted, but it still opens substantial, practical research directions and should be widely built upon.  | B(4): The paper targets an important and practical problem by proposing a unified sentence-pair (f2) framework and evaluating a broad suite of models (CNN, RNN, attention, hybrids) alongside IR baselines across many tasks (wang, yodaqa/large2470, wqmprop, Ubuntu, SNLI, SICK, STS). Its contributions—new harder Answer Sentence Selection datasets (yodaqa/large2470, wqmprop), a statistically grounded evaluation protocol (averaging runs with 95% CIs), and an open-source unified framework—are likely to change evaluation practice and be adopted by researchers. Demonstrated transfer learning benefits (pretraining on Ubuntu Dialogue) further support model reusability, though omissions of some model classes and lagging SOTA on RTE/STS limit the work from being fully transformative.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper addresses an important practical problem by unifying diverse sentence-pair tasks under a single f2 framework and providing an open-source platform (dataset-sts / KeraSTS), new harder Answer Sentence Selection datasets (yodaqa/large2470, wqmprop), and a statistically grounded evaluation protocol (averaging runs with 95% CIs). Its systematic comparison of classical IR baselines and multiple neural architectures, plus the demonstration that transfer learning from the large Ubuntu Dialogue dataset improves performance, will likely change experimental practice and encourage model reuse across tasks. It is not fully transformative because the models do not achieve SOTA on RTE/STS and some model classes/datasets were omitted, but it still opens substantial, practical research directions and should be widely built upon.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper targets an important and practical problem by proposing a unified sentence-pair (f2) framework and evaluating a broad suite of models (CNN, RNN, attention, hybrids) alongside IR baselines across many tasks (wang, yodaqa/large2470, wqmprop, Ubuntu, SNLI, SICK, STS). Its contributions—new harder Answer Sentence Selection datasets (yodaqa/large2470, wqmprop), a statistically grounded evaluation protocol (averaging runs with 95% CIs), and an open-source unified framework—are likely to change evaluation practice and be adopted by researchers. Demonstrated transfer learning benefits (pretraining on Ubuntu Dialogue) further support model reusability, though omissions of some model classes and lagging SOTA on RTE/STS limit the work from being fully transformative.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper’s main claims are supported by extensive experiments across multiple f2 datasets (wang, new yodaqa/large2470 and wqmprop, Ubuntu, SNLI, SICK, STS, etc.), comparisons to IR baselines and several neural architectures, and a statistically grounded evaluation protocol (averaging 16 runs and reporting 95% CIs). The transfer-learning claim is empirically backed by reusing Ubuntu Dialogue-trained weights and observing improved performance on other sentence-pair tasks. Experimental methodology is generally sound (proper baselines, open-source framework, reported hyperparameters), but the work omits several major model classes and some tasks, and the authors note their models lag behind state-of-the-art on RTE and STS — minor limitations that do not invalidate the main conclusions. | B(4): The paper's main empirical claims are well supported: a broad set of neural and IR baselines were implemented and evaluated across many sentence-pair datasets (including newly introduced yodaqa/large2470 and wqmprop), and the authors use averaged runs with 95% CIs to produce more stable measurements. Their experimental methodology is sound in including classical baselines (TF-IDF, BM25), multiple neural architectures, and transfer-learning experiments showing Ubuntu Dialogue pretraining improves downstream performance. Remaining minor technical concerns are acknowledged by the authors (e.g., poorer results on RTE/STS, exclusion of some model classes), so while not flawless, the technical evidence supports the main claims.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper’s main claims are supported by extensive experiments across multiple f2 datasets (wang, new yodaqa/large2470 and wqmprop, Ubuntu, SNLI, SICK, STS, etc.), comparisons to IR baselines and several neural architectures, and a statistically grounded evaluation protocol (averaging 16 runs and reporting 95% CIs). The transfer-learning claim is empirically backed by reusing Ubuntu Dialogue-trained weights and observing improved performance on other sentence-pair tasks. Experimental methodology is generally sound (proper baselines, open-source framework, reported hyperparameters), but the work omits several major model classes and some tasks, and the authors note their models lag behind state-of-the-art on RTE and STS — minor limitations that do not invalidate the main conclusions.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper's main empirical claims are well supported: a broad set of neural and IR baselines were implemented and evaluated across many sentence-pair datasets (including newly introduced yodaqa/large2470 and wqmprop), and the authors use averaged runs with 95% CIs to produce more stable measurements. Their experimental methodology is sound in including classical baselines (TF-IDF, BM25), multiple neural architectures, and transfer-learning experiments showing Ubuntu Dialogue pretraining improves downstream performance. Remaining minor technical concerns are acknowledged by the authors (e.g., poorer results on RTE/STS, exclusion of some model classes), so while not flawless, the technical evidence supports the main claims.

main_claims: Proposes unifying various sentence-pair semantic tasks under a single f2 (sentence pair scoring) framework and argues that task-independent models can be applied across these tasks.
Compares classical IR baselines and multiple neural architectures (CNN, RNN, attention-based, and hybrids) across many sentence-pair tasks and introduces stronger baseline evaluations.

Introduces new, harder Answer Sentence Selection datasets (yodaqa/large2470 and wqmprop), a statistically grounded evaluation methodology (averaging runs and reporting 95% CIs), and an open-source unified software framework for easy model/task integration.
Demonstrates that transfer learning (training on the large Ubuntu Dialogue dataset and reusing weights) improves performance on other sentence-pair tasks, supporting model reusability.
_summary.methodology: They implement and evaluate multiple neural sentence-pair models (avg embeddings, DAN, bidirectional GRU RNN, multi-channel CNN, RNN-CNN hybrid, and an attention model attn1511) and IR baselines (TF-IDF, BM25) across multiple f2-type datasets using a unified open-source framework (dataset-sts / KeraSTS), reporting averaged results over 16 runs and 95% confidence intervals; they also experiment with transfer learning from the Ubuntu Dialogue dataset.
datasets: yodaqa/large2470 (new, derived from curatedv2 and YodaQA)
wqmprop (Property Selection, derived from WebQuestions and moviesE)
baselines: avg (mean word embeddings with projection + MLP)
limitations: Existing popular dataset wang is small and has small/uneven candidate sets causing noisy and unstable ranking measurements and poor validation-test correlation.
Observed heavy overfitting during training; validation performance is not always a good proxy for test performance.
Their models lag behind state-of-the-art on RTE and STS tasks.
They did not include several major model classes (e.g., serial RNNs with attention variants and skip-thoughts) and left some tasks/datasets (e.g., baBi, AskUbuntu, MT QE) for future work.
_summary.topic_area: NLP - sentence pair scoring / text comprehension
